title: Brain

infobox.value: is an [organ](/wiki/Organ_(biology) "Organ (biology)") that serves as the center of the [nervous system](/wiki/Nervous_system "Nervous system") in all [vertebrate](/wiki/Vertebrate "Vertebrate") and most [invertebrate](/wiki/Invertebrate "Invertebrate") [animals](/wiki/Animal "Animal"). It consists of [nervous tissue](/wiki/Nervous_tissue "Nervous tissue") and is typically located in the [head](/wiki/Head "Head") ([cephalization](/wiki/Cephalization "Cephalization")), usually near organs for [special senses](/wiki/Special_senses "Special senses") such as [vision](/wiki/Visual_perception "Visual perception"), [hearing](/wiki/Hearing "Hearing"), and [olfaction](/wiki/Olfaction "Olfaction"). Being the most specialized organ, it is responsible for receiving [information](/wiki/Information "Information") from the [sensory nervous system](/wiki/Sensory_nervous_system "Sensory nervous system"), processing that information ([thought](/wiki/Thought "Thought"), [cognition](/wiki/Cognition "Cognition"), and [intelligence](/wiki/Intelligence "Intelligence")) and the coordination of [motor control](/wiki/Motor_control "Motor control") ([muscle](/wiki/Muscle "Muscle") activity and [endocrine system](/wiki/Endocrine_system "Endocrine system")).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Organ central to the nervous system

This article is about the brains of all types of animals. For information specific to humans, see [human brain](/wiki/Human_brain "Human brain"). For other uses, see [brain (disambiguation)](/wiki/Brain_(disambiguation) "Brain (disambiguation)") and [brains (disambiguation)](/wiki/Brains_(disambiguation) "Brains (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [Brian](/wiki/Brian "Brian"), [braine](/wiki/Braine_(disambiguation) "Braine (disambiguation)"), or [brane](/wiki/Brane "Brane").

The **brain** is an [organ](/wiki/Organ_(biology) "Organ (biology)") that serves as the center of the [nervous system](/wiki/Nervous_system "Nervous system") in all [vertebrate](/wiki/Vertebrate "Vertebrate") and most [invertebrate](/wiki/Invertebrate "Invertebrate") [animals](/wiki/Animal "Animal"). It consists of [nervous tissue](/wiki/Nervous_tissue "Nervous tissue") and is typically located in the [head](/wiki/Head "Head") ([cephalization](/wiki/Cephalization "Cephalization")), usually near organs for [special senses](/wiki/Special_senses "Special senses") such as [vision](/wiki/Visual_perception "Visual perception"), [hearing](/wiki/Hearing "Hearing"), and [olfaction](/wiki/Olfaction "Olfaction"). Being the most specialized organ, it is responsible for receiving [information](/wiki/Information "Information") from the [sensory nervous system](/wiki/Sensory_nervous_system "Sensory nervous system"), processing that information ([thought](/wiki/Thought "Thought"), [cognition](/wiki/Cognition "Cognition"), and [intelligence](/wiki/Intelligence "Intelligence")) and the coordination of [motor control](/wiki/Motor_control "Motor control") ([muscle](/wiki/Muscle "Muscle") activity and [endocrine system](/wiki/Endocrine_system "Endocrine system")).

While invertebrate brains arise from paired [segmental ganglia](/wiki/Segmental_ganglia "Segmental ganglia") (each of which is only responsible for the respective [body segment](/wiki/Segmentation_(biology) "Segmentation (biology)")) of the [ventral nerve cord](/wiki/Ventral_nerve_cord "Ventral nerve cord"), vertebrate brains develop axially from the midline [dorsal nerve cord](/wiki/Dorsal_nerve_cord "Dorsal nerve cord") as a [vesicular](/wiki/Brain_vesicle "Brain vesicle") enlargement at the [rostral](/wiki/Rostral_(anatomical_term) "Rostral (anatomical term)") end of the [neural tube](/wiki/Neural_tube "Neural tube"), with [centralized](/wiki/Centralize "Centralize") control over all body segments. All vertebrate brains can be embryonically divided into three parts: the [forebrain](/wiki/Forebrain "Forebrain") (prosencephalon, subdivided into [telencephalon](/wiki/Telencephalon "Telencephalon") and [diencephalon](/wiki/Diencephalon "Diencephalon")), [midbrain](/wiki/Midbrain "Midbrain") ([mesencephalon](/wiki/Mesencephalon "Mesencephalon")) and [hindbrain](/wiki/Hindbrain "Hindbrain") ([rhombencephalon](/wiki/Rhombencephalon "Rhombencephalon"), subdivided into [metencephalon](/wiki/Metencephalon "Metencephalon") and [myelencephalon](/wiki/Myelencephalon "Myelencephalon")). The [spinal cord](/wiki/Spinal_cord "Spinal cord"), which directly interacts with [somatic](/wiki/Somatic_nervous_system "Somatic nervous system") functions below the head, can be considered a caudal extension of the myelencephalon enclosed inside the [vertebral column](/wiki/Vertebral_column "Vertebral column"). Together, the brain and spinal cord constitute the [central nervous system](/wiki/Central_nervous_system "Central nervous system") in all vertebrates.

In [humans](/wiki/Human "Human"), the [cerebral cortex](/wiki/Cerebral_cortex "Cerebral cortex") contains approximately 14–16 billion neurons,[[1]](#cite_note-Saladin11-1) and the estimated number of neurons in the [cerebellum](/wiki/Cerebellum "Cerebellum") is 55–70 billion.[[2]](#cite_note-JCN-2) Each neuron is connected by [synapses](/wiki/Synapse "Synapse") to several thousand other neurons, typically communicating with one another via [cytoplasmic processes](/wiki/Cytoplasmic_process "Cytoplasmic process") known as [dendrites](/wiki/Dendrite "Dendrite") and [axons](/wiki/Axon "Axon"). Axons are usually [myelinated](/wiki/Myelinated "Myelinated") and carry trains of rapid micro-electric signal pulses called [action potentials](/wiki/Action_potential "Action potential") to target specific recipient cells in other areas of the brain or distant parts of the body. The [prefrontal cortex](/wiki/Prefrontal_cortex "Prefrontal cortex"), which controls [executive functions](/wiki/Executive_function "Executive function"), is particularly well developed in humans.

[Physiologically](/wiki/Physiologically "Physiologically"), brains exert centralized control over a body's other organs. They act on the rest of the body both by generating patterns of muscle activity and by driving the secretion of chemicals called [hormones](/wiki/Hormone "Hormone"). This centralized control allows rapid and coordinated responses to changes in the [environment](/wiki/Natural_environment "Natural environment"). Some basic types of responsiveness such as [reflexes](/wiki/Reflex "Reflex") can be mediated by the spinal cord or peripheral [ganglia](/wiki/Ganglia "Ganglia"), but sophisticated purposeful control of behavior based on complex sensory input requires the information-integrating capabilities of a centralized brain.

The operations of individual brain cells are now understood in considerable detail but the way they cooperate in ensembles of millions is yet to be solved.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Recent models in modern neuroscience treat the brain as a [biological computer](/wiki/Biological_computer "Biological computer"), very different in mechanism from a [digital computer](/wiki/Digital_computer "Digital computer"), but similar in the sense that it acquires information from the surrounding world, stores it, and processes it in a variety of ways.

This article compares the properties of brains across the entire range of animal species, with the greatest attention to vertebrates. It deals with the [human brain](/wiki/Human_brain "Human brain") insofar as it shares the properties of other brains. The ways in which the human brain differs from other brains are covered in the human brain article. Several topics that might be covered here are instead covered there because much more can be said about them in a human context. The most important that are covered in the human brain article are [brain disease](/wiki/Brain_disease "Brain disease") and the effects of [brain damage](/wiki/Brain_damage "Brain damage").

## Structure

[![a blob with a blue patch in the center, surrounded by a white area, surrounded by a thin strip of dark-colored material](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Section_through_olfactory_bulb_16_days_old_rat_brain.jpg/330px-Section_through_olfactory_bulb_16_days_old_rat_brain.jpg)](/wiki/File:Section_through_olfactory_bulb_16_days_old_rat_brain.jpg)

Cross section of the [olfactory bulb](/wiki/Olfactory_bulb "Olfactory bulb") of a [rat](/wiki/Rat "Rat"), [stained](/wiki/Staining "Staining") in two different ways at the same time: one stain shows [neuronal cell bodies](/wiki/Soma_(biology) "Soma (biology)"), the other shows receptors for the [neurotransmitter](/wiki/Neurotransmitter "Neurotransmitter") [GABA](/wiki/GABA "GABA").

The shape and size of the brain varies greatly between species, and identifying common features is often difficult.[[4]](#cite_note-Shepherd-4) Nevertheless, there are a number of principles of brain architecture that apply across a wide range of species.[[5]](#cite_note-Sporns2010-5) Some aspects of brain structure are common to almost the entire range of animal species;[[6]](#cite_note-Başar2010-6) others distinguish "advanced" brains from more primitive ones, or distinguish vertebrates from invertebrates.[[4]](#cite_note-Shepherd-4)

The simplest way to gain information about brain anatomy is by visual inspection, but many more sophisticated techniques have been developed. Brain tissue in its natural state is too soft to work with, but it can be hardened by immersion in alcohol or other [fixatives](/wiki/Fixation_(histology) "Fixation (histology)"), and then sliced apart for examination of the interior. Visually, the interior of the brain consists of areas of so-called [grey matter](/wiki/Grey_matter "Grey matter"), with a dark color, separated by areas of [white matter](/wiki/White_matter "White matter"), with a lighter color. Further information can be gained by staining slices of brain tissue with a variety of chemicals that bring out areas where specific types of molecules are present in high concentrations. It is also possible to [examine the microstructure](/wiki/Histology "Histology") of brain tissue using a microscope, and to trace the pattern of connections from one brain area to another.[[7]](#cite_note-Singh2006-7)

### Cellular structure

[![drawing showing a neuron with a fiber emanating from it labeled "axon" and making contact with another cell. An inset shows an enlargement of the contact zone.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Chemical_synapse_schema_cropped.jpg/330px-Chemical_synapse_schema_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chemical_synapse_schema_cropped.jpg)

[Neurons](/wiki/Neuron "Neuron") generate [electrical signals](/wiki/Action_potential "Action potential") that travel along their [axons](/wiki/Axon "Axon"). When an electrical impulse reaches a junction called a [synapse](/wiki/Synapse "Synapse"), it causes a [neurotransmitter](/wiki/Neurotransmitter "Neurotransmitter") to be released, which binds to [receptors](/wiki/Receptor_(biochemistry) "Receptor (biochemistry)") on other cells and thereby alters their electrical activity.

The brains of all species are composed primarily of two broad classes of [brain cells](/wiki/Brain_cell "Brain cell"): [neurons](/wiki/Neuron "Neuron") and [glial cells](/wiki/Neuroglia "Neuroglia"). Glial cells (also known as *glia* or *neuroglia*) come in several types, and perform a number of critical functions, including structural support, metabolic support, insulation, and guidance of development. Neurons, however, are usually considered the most important cells in the brain.[[8]](#cite_note-Kandel_2000-8) In humans, the [cerebral cortex](/wiki/Cerebral_cortex "Cerebral cortex") contains approximately 14–16 billion neurons,[[1]](#cite_note-Saladin11-1) and the estimated number of neurons in the [cerebellum](/wiki/Cerebellum "Cerebellum") is 55–70 billion.[[2]](#cite_note-JCN-2) Each neuron is connected by [synapses](/wiki/Synapse "Synapse") to several thousand other neurons. The property that makes neurons unique is their ability to send signals to specific target cells, sometimes over long distances.[[8]](#cite_note-Kandel_2000-8) They send these signals by means of an [axon](/wiki/Axon "Axon"), which is a thin protoplasmic fiber that extends from the cell body and projects, usually with numerous branches, to other areas, sometimes nearby, sometimes in distant parts of the brain or body. The length of an axon can be extraordinary: for example, if a [pyramidal cell](/wiki/Pyramidal_cell "Pyramidal cell") (an excitatory neuron) of the cerebral cortex were magnified so that its cell body became the size of a human body, its axon, equally magnified, would become a cable a few centimeters in diameter, extending more than a kilometer.[[9]](#cite_note-9) These axons transmit signals in the form of electrochemical pulses called action potentials, which last less than a thousandth of a second and travel along the axon at speeds of 1–100 meters per second. Some neurons emit action potentials constantly, at rates of 10–100 per second, usually in irregular patterns; other neurons are quiet most of the time, but occasionally emit a burst of action potentials.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Axons transmit signals to other neurons by means of specialized junctions called [synapses](/wiki/Synapse "Synapse"). A single axon may make as many as several thousand synaptic connections with other cells.[[8]](#cite_note-Kandel_2000-8) When an action potential, traveling along an axon, arrives at a synapse, it causes a chemical called a [neurotransmitter](/wiki/Neurotransmitter "Neurotransmitter") to be released. The neurotransmitter binds to [receptor](/wiki/Receptor_(biochemistry) "Receptor (biochemistry)") molecules in the membrane of the target cell.[[8]](#cite_note-Kandel_2000-8)

Synapses are the key functional elements of the brain.[[11]](#cite_note-ShepherdSOB-11) The essential function of the brain is [cell-to-cell communication](/wiki/Cell%E2%80%93cell_interaction "Cell–cell interaction"), and synapses are the points at which communication occurs. The human brain has been estimated to contain approximately 100 trillion synapses;[[12]](#cite_note-12) even the brain of a fruit fly contains several million.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The functions of these synapses are very diverse: some are excitatory (exciting the target cell); others are inhibitory; others work by activating [second messenger systems](/wiki/Second_messenger_system "Second messenger system") that change the internal [chemistry](/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry") of their target cells in complex ways.[[11]](#cite_note-ShepherdSOB-11) A large number of synapses are dynamically modifiable; that is, they are capable of changing strength in a way that is controlled by the patterns of signals that pass through them. It is widely believed that [activity-dependent modification of synapses](/wiki/Synaptic_plasticity "Synaptic plasticity") is the brain's primary mechanism for learning and memory.[[11]](#cite_note-ShepherdSOB-11)

Most of the space in the brain is taken up by axons, which are often bundled together in what are called *nerve fiber tracts*. A myelinated axon is wrapped in a fatty insulating sheath of [myelin](/wiki/Myelin "Myelin"), which serves to greatly increase the speed of signal propagation. (There are also unmyelinated axons). Myelin is white, making parts of the brain filled exclusively with nerve fibers appear as light-colored [white matter](/wiki/White_matter "White matter"), in contrast to the darker-colored [grey matter](/wiki/Grey_matter "Grey matter") that marks areas with high densities of neuron cell bodies.[[8]](#cite_note-Kandel_2000-8)

## Evolution

Main article: [Evolution of the brain](/wiki/Evolution_of_the_brain "Evolution of the brain")

### Generic bilaterian nervous system

[![A rod-shaped body contains a digestive system running from the mouth at one end to the anus at the other. Alongside the digestive system is a nerve cord with a brain at the end, near to the mouth.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Bilaterian-plan.svg/330px-Bilaterian-plan.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Bilaterian-plan.svg)

Nervous system of a generic bilaterian animal, in the form of a nerve cord with segmental enlargements, and a "brain" at the front

Except for a few primitive organisms such as [sponges](/wiki/Sponge "Sponge") (which have no nervous system)[[14]](#cite_note-Jacobs-14) and [cnidarians](/wiki/Cnidaria "Cnidaria") (which have a diffuse nervous system consisting of a [nerve net](/wiki/Nerve_net "Nerve net")),[[14]](#cite_note-Jacobs-14) all living multicellular animals are [bilaterians](/wiki/Bilateria "Bilateria"), meaning animals with a [bilaterally symmetric](/wiki/Bilateral_symmetry "Bilateral symmetry") [body plan](/wiki/Body_plan "Body plan") (that is, left and right sides that are approximate mirror images of each other).[[15]](#cite_note-Urbilateria-15) All bilaterians are thought to have descended from a common ancestor that appeared late in the [Cryogenian](/wiki/Cryogenian "Cryogenian") period, 700–650 million years ago, and it has been hypothesized that this common ancestor had the shape of a simple tubeworm with a segmented body.[[15]](#cite_note-Urbilateria-15) At a schematic level, that basic worm-shape continues to be reflected in the body and nervous system architecture of all modern bilaterians, including vertebrates.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The fundamental bilateral body form is a tube with a hollow gut cavity running from the mouth to the anus, and a nerve cord with an enlargement (a [ganglion](/wiki/Ganglion "Ganglion")) for each body segment, with an especially large ganglion at the front, called the brain. The brain is small and simple in some species, such as [nematode](/wiki/Nematode "Nematode") worms; in other species, such as vertebrates, it is a large and very complex organ.[[4]](#cite_note-Shepherd-4) Some types of worms, such as [leeches](/wiki/Leech "Leech"), also have an enlarged ganglion at the back end of the nerve cord, known as a "tail brain".[[17]](#cite_note-17)

There are a few types of existing bilaterians that lack a recognizable brain, including [echinoderms](/wiki/Echinoderm "Echinoderm") and [tunicates](/wiki/Tunicate "Tunicate"). It has not been definitively established whether the existence of these brainless species indicates that the earliest [bilaterians](/wiki/Bilaterians "Bilaterians") lacked a brain, or whether their ancestors evolved in a way that led to the disappearance of a previously existing brain structure.

### Invertebrates

[![A fly resting on a reflective surface. A large, red eye faces the camera. The body appears transparent, apart from black pigment at the end of its abdomen.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Drosophila_melanogaster_-_side_%28aka%29.jpg/250px-Drosophila_melanogaster_-_side_%28aka%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Drosophila_melanogaster_-_side_(aka).jpg)

Fruit flies (*[Drosophila](/wiki/Drosophila "Drosophila")*) have been extensively studied to gain insight into the role of genes in brain development.

This category includes [tardigrades](/wiki/Tardigrade "Tardigrade"), [arthropods](/wiki/Arthropod "Arthropod"), [molluscs](/wiki/Mollusca "Mollusca"), and numerous types of worms. The diversity of invertebrate body plans is matched by an equal diversity in brain structures.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Two groups of invertebrates have notably complex brains: arthropods (insects, [crustaceans](/wiki/Crustacean "Crustacean"), [arachnids](/wiki/Arachnid "Arachnid"), and others), and [cephalopods](/wiki/Cephalopod "Cephalopod") (octopuses, [squids](/wiki/Squid "Squid"), and similar molluscs).[[19]](#cite_note-Butler-19) The brains of arthropods and cephalopods arise from twin parallel nerve cords that extend through the body of the animal. Arthropods have a central brain, the [supraesophageal ganglion](/wiki/Supraesophageal_ganglion "Supraesophageal ganglion"), with three divisions and large [optical lobes](/wiki/Optic_lobe_(arthropod) "Optic lobe (arthropod)") behind each eye for visual processing.[[19]](#cite_note-Butler-19) Cephalopods such as the octopus and squid have the largest brains of any invertebrates.[[20]](#cite_note-Bulloch1995-20)

There are several invertebrate species whose brains have been studied intensively because they have properties that make them convenient for experimental work:

* Fruit flies (*Drosophila*), because of the large array of techniques available for studying their [genetics](/wiki/Genetics "Genetics"), have been a natural subject for studying the role of genes in brain development.[[21]](#cite_note-21) In spite of the large evolutionary distance between insects and mammals, many aspects of *Drosophila* [neurogenetics](/wiki/Neurogenetics "Neurogenetics") have been shown to be relevant to humans. The first biological [clock genes](/wiki/Clock_gene "Clock gene"), for example, were identified by examining *Drosophila* mutants that showed disrupted daily activity cycles.[[22]](#cite_note-22) A search in the genomes of vertebrates revealed a set of analogous genes, which were found to play similar roles in the mouse biological clock—and therefore almost certainly in the human biological clock as well.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Studies done on Drosophila, also show that most [neuropil](/wiki/Neuropil "Neuropil") regions of the brain are continuously reorganized throughout life in response to specific living conditions.[[24]](#cite_note-24)
* The nematode worm *[Caenorhabditis elegans](/wiki/Caenorhabditis_elegans "Caenorhabditis elegans")*, like *Drosophila*, has been studied largely because of its importance in genetics.[[25]](#cite_note-25) In the early 1970s, [Sydney Brenner](/wiki/Sydney_Brenner "Sydney Brenner") chose it as a [model organism](/wiki/Model_organism "Model organism") for studying the way that genes control development. One of the advantages of working with this worm is that the body plan is very stereotyped: the nervous system of the [hermaphrodite](/wiki/Hermaphrodite "Hermaphrodite") contains exactly 302 neurons, always in the same places, making identical synaptic connections in every worm.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Brenner's team sliced worms into thousands of ultrathin sections and photographed each one under an electron microscope, then visually matched fibers from section to section, to map out every neuron and synapse in the entire body.[[27]](#cite_note-27) The complete neuronal *wiring diagram* of *C.elegans* – its [connectome](/wiki/Connectome "Connectome") was achieved.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Nothing approaching this level of detail is available for any other organism, and the information gained has enabled a multitude of studies that would otherwise have not been possible.[[29]](#cite_note-29)
* The sea slug *[Aplysia](/wiki/Aplysia "Aplysia") californica* was chosen by Nobel Prize-winning neurophysiologist [Eric Kandel](/wiki/Eric_Kandel "Eric Kandel") as a model for studying the cellular basis of [learning](/wiki/Learning "Learning") and [memory](/wiki/Memory "Memory"), because of the simplicity and accessibility of its nervous system, and it has been examined in hundreds of experiments.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Vertebrates

[![A T-shaped object is made up of the cord at the bottom which feeds into a lower central mass. This is topped by a larger central mass with an arm extending from either side.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Shark_brain.png/250px-Shark_brain.png)](/wiki/File:Shark_brain.png)

The brain of a [shark](/wiki/Shark "Shark")

The first [vertebrates](/wiki/Vertebrate "Vertebrate") appeared over 500 million years ago ([Mya](/wiki/Mya_(unit) "Mya (unit)")) during the [Cambrian period](/wiki/Cambrian_period "Cambrian period"), and may have resembled the modern [jawless fish](/wiki/Jawless_fish "Jawless fish") ([hagfish](/wiki/Hagfish "Hagfish") and [lamprey](/wiki/Lamprey "Lamprey")) in form.[[31]](#cite_note-31) [Jawed vertebrates](/wiki/Jawed_vertebrate "Jawed vertebrate") appeared by 445 Mya, [tetrapods](/wiki/Tetrapod "Tetrapod") by 350 Mya, [amniotes](/wiki/Amniote "Amniote") by 310 Mya and [mammaliaforms](/wiki/Mammaliaform "Mammaliaform") by 200 Mya (approximately). Each vertebrate [clade](/wiki/Clade "Clade") has an equally long [evolutionary](/wiki/Evolution "Evolution") history, but the brains of modern [fish](/wiki/Fish "Fish"), [amphibians](/wiki/Amphibian "Amphibian"), [reptiles](/wiki/Reptile "Reptile"), [birds](/wiki/Bird "Bird") and [mammals](/wiki/Mammal "Mammal") show a gradient of size and complexity that roughly follows the evolutionary sequence. All of these brains contain the same set of basic anatomical structures, but many are rudimentary in the hagfish, whereas in mammals the foremost part ([forebrain](/wiki/Forebrain "Forebrain"), especially the [telencephalon](/wiki/Telencephalon "Telencephalon")) is greatly developed and expanded.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Brains are most commonly compared in terms of their [mass](/wiki/Mass "Mass"). The [relationship](/wiki/Encephalization_quotient "Encephalization quotient") between [brain size](/wiki/Brain_size "Brain size"), body size and other variables has been studied across a wide range of vertebrate species. As a [rule of thumb](/wiki/Rule_of_thumb "Rule of thumb"), brain size increases with body size, but not in a simple linear proportion. In general, smaller animals tend to have proportionally larger brains, measured as a fraction of body size. For mammals, the relationship between brain volume and body mass essentially follows a [power law](/wiki/Power_law "Power law") with an [exponent](/wiki/Exponentiation "Exponentiation") of about 0.75.[[33]](#cite_note-33) This formula describes the central tendency, but every family of mammals departs from it to some degree, in a way that reflects in part the complexity of their behavior. For example, [primates](/wiki/Primate "Primate") have brains 5 to 10 times larger than the formula predicts. [Predators](/wiki/Predator "Predator"), who have to implement various [hunting strategies](/wiki/Hunting_strategies "Hunting strategies") against the [ever changing](/wiki/Evolutionary_arms_race "Evolutionary arms race") [anti-predator adaptations](/wiki/Anti-predator_adaptation "Anti-predator adaptation"), tend to have larger brains relative to body size than their prey.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

[![The nervous system is shown as a rod with protrusions along its length. The spinal cord at the bottom connects to the hindbrain which widens out before narrowing again. This is connected to the midbrain, which again bulges, and which finally connects to the forebrain which has two large protrusions.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/EmbryonicBrain.svg/250px-EmbryonicBrain.svg.png)](/wiki/File:EmbryonicBrain.svg)

The main subdivisions of the [embryonic](/wiki/Embryogenesis "Embryogenesis") vertebrate brain (left), which later differentiate into structures of the adult brain (right)

All vertebrate brains share a common underlying form, which appears most clearly during early stages of [embryonic development](/wiki/Embryonic_development "Embryonic development"). In its earliest form, the brain appears as three [vesicular swellings](/wiki/Brain_vesicle "Brain vesicle") at the front end of the [neural tube](/wiki/Neural_tube "Neural tube"); these swellings eventually become the [forebrain](/wiki/Forebrain "Forebrain") ([prosencephalon](/wiki/Prosencephalon "Prosencephalon")), [midbrain](/wiki/Midbrain "Midbrain") ([mesencephalon](/wiki/Mesencephalon "Mesencephalon")) and hindbrain ([rhombencephalon](/wiki/Rhombencephalon "Rhombencephalon")), respectively. At the earliest stages of brain development, the three areas are roughly equal in size. In many [aquatic](/wiki/Aquatic_animal "Aquatic animal")/[semiaquatic](/wiki/Semiaquatic "Semiaquatic") vertebrates such as fish and amphibians, the three parts remain similar in size in [adults](/wiki/Adult "Adult"), but in [terrestrial](/wiki/Terrestrial_animal "Terrestrial animal") [tetrapods](/wiki/Tetrapod "Tetrapod") such as mammals, the forebrain becomes much larger than the other parts, the hindbrain develops a bulky [dorsal](/wiki/Dorsal_(anatomy) "Dorsal (anatomy)") extension known as the [cerebellum](/wiki/Cerebellum "Cerebellum"), and the midbrain becomes very small as a result.[[8]](#cite_note-Kandel_2000-8)

The brains of vertebrates are made of very soft tissue.[[8]](#cite_note-Kandel_2000-8) Living brain tissue is pinkish on the outside and mostly white on the inside, with subtle variations in color. Vertebrate brains are surrounded by a system of [connective tissue](/wiki/Connective_tissue "Connective tissue") [membranes](/wiki/Biological_membrane "Biological membrane") called [meninges](/wiki/Meninges "Meninges"), which separate the [skull](/wiki/Skull "Skull") from the brain. [Cerebral arteries](/wiki/Cerebral_arteries "Cerebral arteries") pierce the outer two layers of the meninges, the [dura](/wiki/Dura_mater "Dura mater") and [arachnoid mater](/wiki/Arachnoid_mater "Arachnoid mater"), into the [subarachnoid space](/wiki/Subarachnoid_space "Subarachnoid space") and [perfuse](/wiki/Perfusion "Perfusion") the [brain parenchyma](/wiki/Brain_parenchyma "Brain parenchyma") via [arterioles](/wiki/Arteriole "Arteriole") perforating into the innermost layer of the meninges, the [pia mater](/wiki/Pia_mater "Pia mater"). The [endothelial cells](/wiki/Endothelial_cell "Endothelial cell") in the cerebral blood vessel walls are joined tightly to one another, forming the [blood–brain barrier](/wiki/Blood%E2%80%93brain_barrier "Blood–brain barrier"), which blocks the passage of many [toxins](/wiki/Toxin "Toxin") and [pathogens](/wiki/Pathogen "Pathogen")[[35]](#cite_note-CarpenterCh1-35) (though at the same time blocking [antibodies](/wiki/Antibody "Antibody") and some drugs, thereby presenting special challenges in treatment of diseases of the brain).[[36]](#cite_note-36) As a result of the [osmotic](/wiki/Osmotic "Osmotic") restriction by the blood-brain barrier, the [metabolites](/wiki/Metabolite "Metabolite") within the brain are cleared mostly by [bulk flow](/wiki/Bulk_flow "Bulk flow") of the [cerebrospinal fluid](/wiki/Cerebrospinal_fluid "Cerebrospinal fluid") within the [glymphatic system](/wiki/Glymphatic_system "Glymphatic system") instead of via [venules](/wiki/Venule "Venule") like other parts of the body.

[Neuroanatomists](/wiki/Neuroanatomy "Neuroanatomy") usually divide the vertebrate brain into six main subregions: the [telencephalon](/wiki/Telencephalon "Telencephalon") (the [cerebral hemispheres](/wiki/Cerebral_hemispheres "Cerebral hemispheres")), [diencephalon](/wiki/Diencephalon "Diencephalon") ([thalamus](/wiki/Thalamus "Thalamus") and [hypothalamus](/wiki/Hypothalamus "Hypothalamus")), [mesencephalon](/wiki/Mesencephalon "Mesencephalon") (midbrain), [cerebellum](/wiki/Cerebellum "Cerebellum"), [pons](/wiki/Pons "Pons") and [medulla oblongata](/wiki/Medulla_oblongata "Medulla oblongata"), with the midbrain, pons and medulla often collectively called the [brainstem](/wiki/Brainstem "Brainstem"). Each of these areas has a complex internal structure. Some parts, such as the [cerebral cortex](/wiki/Cerebral_cortex "Cerebral cortex") and the cerebellar cortex, are folded into convoluted [gyri](/wiki/Gyri "Gyri") and [sulci](/wiki/Sulcus_(neuroanatomy) "Sulcus (neuroanatomy)") in order to maximize [surface area](/wiki/Surface_area "Surface area") within the available [intracranial space](/wiki/Intracranial_space "Intracranial space"). Other parts, such as the thalamus and hypothalamus, consist of many small clusters of nuclei known as "ganglia". Thousands of distinguishable areas can be identified within the vertebrate brain based on fine distinctions of neural structure, chemistry, and connectivity.[[8]](#cite_note-Kandel_2000-8)

[![Corresponding regions of human and shark brain are shown. The shark brain is splayed out, while the human brain is more compact. The shark brain starts with the medulla, which is surrounded by various structures, and ends with the telencephalon. The cross-section of the human brain shows the medulla at the bottom surrounded by the same structures, with the telencephalon thickly coating the top of the brain.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Vertebrate-brain-regions_small.png/250px-Vertebrate-brain-regions_small.png)](/wiki/File:Vertebrate-brain-regions_small.png)

The main anatomical regions of the vertebrate brain, shown for shark and human. The same parts are present, but they differ greatly in size and shape.

Although the same basic components are present in all vertebrate brains, some branches of vertebrate evolution have led to substantial distortions of brain geometry, especially in the forebrain area. The brain of a shark shows the basic components in a straightforward way, but in [teleost](/wiki/Teleostei "Teleostei") fishes (the great majority of existing fish species), the forebrain has become "everted", like a sock turned inside out. In birds, there are also major changes in forebrain structure.[[37]](#cite_note-37) These distortions can make it difficult to match brain components from one species with those of another species.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Here is a list of some of the most important vertebrate brain components, along with a brief description of their functions as currently understood:

See also: [List of regions in the human brain](/wiki/List_of_regions_in_the_human_brain "List of regions in the human brain")

* The [medulla](/wiki/Medulla_oblongata "Medulla oblongata"), along with the spinal cord, contains many small nuclei involved in a wide variety of sensory and involuntary motor functions such as vomiting, heart rate and digestive processes.[[8]](#cite_note-Kandel_2000-8)
* The [pons](/wiki/Pons "Pons") lies in the brainstem directly above the medulla. Among other things, it contains nuclei that control often voluntary but simple acts such as sleep, respiration, swallowing, bladder function, equilibrium, eye movement, facial expressions, and posture.[[39]](#cite_note-39)
* The [hypothalamus](/wiki/Hypothalamus "Hypothalamus") is a small region at the base of the forebrain, whose complexity and importance belies its size. It is composed of numerous small nuclei, each with distinct connections and neurochemistry. The hypothalamus is engaged in additional involuntary or partially voluntary acts such as sleep and wake cycles, eating and drinking, and the release of some hormones.[[40]](#cite_note-40)
* The [thalamus](/wiki/Thalamus "Thalamus") is a collection of nuclei with diverse functions: some are involved in relaying information to and from the cerebral hemispheres, while others are involved in motivation. The subthalamic area ([zona incerta](/wiki/Zona_incerta "Zona incerta")) seems to contain action-generating systems for several types of "consummatory" behaviors such as eating, drinking, defecation, and copulation.[[41]](#cite_note-41)
* The [cerebellum](/wiki/Cerebellum "Cerebellum") modulates the outputs of other brain systems, whether motor-related or thought related, to make them certain and precise. Removal of the cerebellum does not prevent an animal from doing anything in particular, but it makes actions hesitant and clumsy. This precision is not built-in but learned by trial and error. The muscle coordination learned while riding a bicycle is an example of a type of [neural plasticity](/wiki/Neuroplasticity "Neuroplasticity") that may take place largely within the cerebellum.[[8]](#cite_note-Kandel_2000-8) 10% of the brain's total volume consists of the cerebellum and 50% of all neurons are held within its structure.[[42]](#cite_note-42)
* The [optic tectum](/wiki/Superior_colliculus "Superior colliculus") allows actions to be directed toward points in space, most commonly in response to visual input. In mammals, it is usually referred to as the [superior colliculus](/wiki/Superior_colliculus "Superior colliculus"), and its best-studied function is to direct eye movements. It also directs reaching movements and other object-directed actions. It receives strong visual inputs, but also inputs from other senses that are useful in directing actions, such as auditory input in owls and input from the thermosensitive [pit organs](/wiki/Pit_organ "Pit organ") in snakes. In some primitive fishes, such as [lampreys](/wiki/Lamprey "Lamprey"), this region is the largest part of the brain.[[43]](#cite_note-43) The superior colliculus is part of the midbrain.
* The [pallium](/wiki/Pallium_(neuroanatomy) "Pallium (neuroanatomy)") is a layer of grey matter that lies on the surface of the forebrain and is the most complex and most recent evolutionary development of the brain as an organ.[[44]](#cite_note-LullFerris1922-44) In reptiles and mammals, it is called the *cerebral cortex*. Multiple functions involve the pallium, including [smell](/wiki/Olfaction "Olfaction") and [spatial memory](/wiki/Spatial_memory "Spatial memory"). In mammals, where it becomes so large as to dominate the brain, it takes over functions from many other brain areas. In many mammals, the cerebral cortex consists of folded bulges called [gyri](/wiki/Gyrus "Gyrus") that create deep furrows or fissures called [sulci](/wiki/Sulcus_(neuroanatomy) "Sulcus (neuroanatomy)"). The folds increase the surface area of the cortex and therefore increase the amount of gray matter and the amount of information that can be stored and processed.[[45]](#cite_note-45)
* The [hippocampus](/wiki/Hippocampus "Hippocampus"), strictly speaking, is found only in mammals. However, the area it derives from, the medial pallium, has counterparts in all vertebrates. There is evidence that this part of the brain is involved in complex events such as spatial memory and navigation in fishes, birds, reptiles, and mammals.[[46]](#cite_note-46)
* The [basal ganglia](/wiki/Basal_ganglia "Basal ganglia") are a group of interconnected structures in the forebrain. The primary function of the basal ganglia appears to be [action selection](/wiki/Action_selection "Action selection"): they send inhibitory signals to all parts of the brain that can generate motor behaviors, and in the right circumstances can release the inhibition, so that the action-generating systems are able to execute their actions. Reward and punishment exert their most important neural effects by altering connections within the basal ganglia.[[47]](#cite_note-Grillner2005-47)
* The [olfactory bulb](/wiki/Olfactory_bulb "Olfactory bulb") is a special structure that processes olfactory sensory signals and sends its output to the olfactory part of the pallium. It is a major brain component in many vertebrates, but is greatly reduced in humans and other primates (whose senses are dominated by information acquired by sight rather than smell).[[48]](#cite_note-48)

#### Reptiles

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Comparative_zoology%2C_structural_and_systematic_-_for_use_in_schools_and_colleges_%281883%29_%2820482913440%29.jpg/500px-Comparative_zoology%2C_structural_and_systematic_-_for_use_in_schools_and_colleges_%281883%29_%2820482913440%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Comparative_zoology,_structural_and_systematic_-_for_use_in_schools_and_colleges_(1883)_(20482913440).jpg)

Anatomical comparison between the brain of a lizard (A and C) and the brain of a turkey (B and D). Abbreviations: *Olf, olfactory lobes; Hmp, cerebral hemispheres; Pn, pineal gland; Mb, optic lobes of the middle brain; Cb, cerebellum; MO, medulla oblongata; ii, optic nerves; iv and vi, nerves for the muscles of the eye; Py, pituitary body.*[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Origin_of_Vertebrates_Fig_019.png/330px-Origin_of_Vertebrates_Fig_019.png)](/wiki/File:Origin_of_Vertebrates_Fig_019.png)



Comparison of Vertebrate Brains: Mammalian, Reptilian, Amphibian, Teleost, and Ammocoetes. *CB., cerebellum; PT., pituitary body; PN., pineal body; C. STR., corpus striatum; G.H.R., right ganglion habenulæ. I., olfactory; II., optic nerves.*

Modern [reptiles](/wiki/Reptile "Reptile") and [mammals](/wiki/Mammal "Mammal") diverged from a common ancestor around 320 million years ago.[[49]](#cite_note-:1-49) The number of extant reptiles far exceeds the number of mammalian species, with 11,733 recognized species of reptiles[[50]](#cite_note-50) compared to 5,884 extant mammals.[[51]](#cite_note-51) Along with the species diversity, reptiles have diverged in terms of external morphology, from [limbless](/wiki/Slender_glass_lizard "Slender glass lizard") to [tetrapod gliders](/wiki/Draco_(lizard) "Draco (lizard)") to [armored chelonians](/wiki/Turtle "Turtle"), reflecting adaptive radiation to a diverse array of environments.[[52]](#cite_note-:9-52)[[53]](#cite_note-:0-53)

Morphological differences are reflected in the nervous system [phenotype](/wiki/Phenotype "Phenotype"), such as: absence of lateral motor column neurons in snakes, which innervate limb muscles controlling limb movements; absence of motor neurons that innervate trunk muscles in tortoises; presence of innervation from the trigeminal nerve to [pit organs](/wiki/Infrared_sensing_in_snakes "Infrared sensing in snakes") responsible to infrared detection in snakes.[[52]](#cite_note-:9-52) Variation in size, weight, and shape of the brain can be found within reptiles.[[54]](#cite_note-:6-54) For instance, crocodilians have the largest brain volume to body weight proportion, followed by turtles, lizards, and snakes. Reptiles vary in the investment in different brain sections. Crocodilians have the largest telencephalon, while snakes have the smallest. Turtles have the largest diencephalon per body weight whereas crocodilians have the smallest. On the other hand, lizards have the largest mesencephalon.[[54]](#cite_note-:6-54)

Yet their brains share several characteristics revealed by recent anatomical, molecular, and [ontogenetic](/wiki/Ontogeny "Ontogeny") studies.[[55]](#cite_note-:3-55)[[56]](#cite_note-:4-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57) Vertebrates share the highest levels of similarities during [embryological](/wiki/Embryology "Embryology") development, controlled by [conserved](/wiki/Conserved_sequence "Conserved sequence") [transcription factors](/wiki/Transcription_factor "Transcription factor") and [signaling centers](/wiki/Developmental_signaling_center "Developmental signaling center"), including gene expression, morphological and cell type differentiation.[[55]](#cite_note-:3-55)[[52]](#cite_note-:9-52)[[58]](#cite_note-58) In fact, high levels of transcriptional factors can be found in all areas of the brain in reptiles and mammals, with shared neuronal clusters enlightening brain evolution.[[56]](#cite_note-:4-56) Conserved transcription factors elucidate that evolution acted in different areas of the brain by either retaining similar morphology and function, or diversifying it.[[55]](#cite_note-:3-55)[[56]](#cite_note-:4-56)

Anatomically, the reptilian brain has less subdivisions than the mammalian brain, however it has numerous conserved aspects including the organization of the spinal cord and cranial nerve, as well as elaborated brain pattern of organization.[[59]](#cite_note-:5-59) Elaborated brains are characterized by migrated neuronal cell bodies away from the periventricular matrix, region of neuronal development, forming organized nuclear groups.[[59]](#cite_note-:5-59) Aside from [reptiles](/wiki/Reptile "Reptile") and [mammals](/wiki/Mammal "Mammal"), other vertebrates with elaborated brains include [hagfish](/wiki/Hagfish "Hagfish"), [galeomorph sharks](/wiki/Shark "Shark"), [skates](/wiki/Skate_(fish) "Skate (fish)"), [rays](/wiki/Batoidea "Batoidea"), [teleosts](/wiki/Teleost "Teleost"), and [birds](/wiki/Bird "Bird").[[59]](#cite_note-:5-59) Overall elaborated brains are subdivided in forebrain, midbrain, and hindbrain.

The hindbrain coordinates and integrates sensory and motor inputs and outputs responsible for, but not limited to, walking, swimming, or flying. It contains input and output axons interconnecting the spinal cord, midbrain and forebrain transmitting information from the external and internal environments.[[59]](#cite_note-:5-59) The midbrain links sensory, motor, and integrative components received from the hindbrain, connecting it to the forebrain. The tectum, which includes the optic tectum and torus semicircularis, receives auditory, visual, and somatosensory inputs, forming integrated maps of the sensory and visual space around the animal.[[59]](#cite_note-:5-59) The tegmentum receives incoming sensory information and forwards motor responses to and from the forebrain. The isthmus connects the hindbrain with midbrain. The forebrain region is particularly well developed, is further divided into diencephalon and telencephalon. Diencephalon is related to regulation of eye and body movement in response to visual stimuli, sensory information, [circadian rhythms](/wiki/Circadian_rhythm "Circadian rhythm"), olfactory input, and [autonomic nervous system](/wiki/Autonomic_nervous_system "Autonomic nervous system").Telencephalon is related to control of movements, neurotransmitters and neuromodulators responsible for integrating inputs and transmitting outputs are present, sensory systems, and cognitive functions.[[59]](#cite_note-:5-59)

#### Birds

Main article: [Avian brain](/wiki/Avian_brain "Avian brain")

This section is an excerpt from [Avian brain](/wiki/Avian_brain "Avian brain").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Avian_brain&action=edit)]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Visual_processing_areas_of_the_brains_of_four_species_of_birds.png/330px-Visual_processing_areas_of_the_brains_of_four_species_of_birds.png)](/wiki/File:Visual_processing_areas_of_the_brains_of_four_species_of_birds.png)

Brains of an [emu](/wiki/Emu "Emu"), a [kiwi](/wiki/Kiwi_(bird) "Kiwi (bird)"), a [barn owl](/wiki/Barn-owl "Barn-owl"), and a [pigeon](/wiki/Rock_dove "Rock dove"), with visual processing areas labelled

The [avian brain](/wiki/Avian_brain "Avian brain") is the central organ of the [nervous system](/wiki/Nervous_system "Nervous system") in birds. Birds possess large, complex brains, which [process](/wiki/Information_processing_(psychology) "Information processing (psychology)"), [integrate](/wiki/Information_integration "Information integration"), and coordinate information received from the environment and [make decisions](/wiki/Decision-making "Decision-making") on how to respond with [the rest of the body.](/wiki/Bird_anatomy "Bird anatomy") Like in all [chordates](/wiki/Chordate "Chordate"), the avian brain is contained within the [skull](/wiki/Skull "Skull") bones of the [head](/wiki/Head "Head").

The bird brain is divided into a number of sections, each with a different function. The [cerebrum](/wiki/Cerebrum "Cerebrum") or telencephalon is divided into two [hemispheres](/wiki/Cerebral_hemisphere "Cerebral hemisphere"), and controls higher functions. The telencephalon is dominated by a large [pallium](/wiki/Avian_pallium "Avian pallium"), which corresponds to the [mammalian](/wiki/Mammal "Mammal") [cerebral cortex](/wiki/Cerebral_cortex "Cerebral cortex") and is responsible for the [cognitive functions](/wiki/Cognitive_skill "Cognitive skill") of birds. The pallium is made up of several major structures: the hyperpallium, a dorsal bulge of the pallium found only in birds, as well as the nidopallium, mesopallium, and archipallium. The bird telencephalon nuclear structure, wherein neurons are distributed in three-dimensionally arranged clusters, with no large-scale separation of [white matter](/wiki/White_matter "White matter") and [grey matter](/wiki/Grey_matter "Grey matter"), though there exist layer-like and column-like connections. Structures in the pallium are associated with [perception](/wiki/Perception "Perception"), [learning](/wiki/Learning "Learning"), and [cognition](/wiki/Cognition "Cognition"). Beneath the pallium are the two components of the subpallium, the [striatum](/wiki/Striatum "Striatum") and [pallidum](/wiki/Globus_pallidus "Globus pallidus"). The subpallium connects different parts of the telencephalon and plays major roles in a number of critical behaviours. To the rear of the telencephalon are the [thalamus](/wiki/Thalamus "Thalamus"), [midbrain](/wiki/Midbrain "Midbrain"), and [cerebellum](/wiki/Cerebellum "Cerebellum"). The [hindbrain](/wiki/Hindbrain "Hindbrain") connects the rest of the brain to the spinal cord.

The size and structure of the avian brain enables prominent behaviours of birds such as [flight](/wiki/Bird_flight "Bird flight") and [vocalization](/wiki/Bird_vocalization "Bird vocalization"). Dedicated structures and pathways integrate the [auditory](/wiki/Hearing "Hearing") and [visual](/wiki/Bird_vision "Bird vision") senses, strong in most species of birds, as well as the typically weaker [olfactory](/wiki/Sense_of_smell "Sense of smell") and [tactile](/wiki/Somatosensory_system "Somatosensory system") senses. [Social behaviour](/wiki/Social_behavior "Social behavior"), widespread among birds, depends on the organisation and functions of the brain. Some birds exhibit strong abilities of cognition, enabled by the unique structure and physiology of the avian brain.

#### Mammals

The most obvious difference between the brains of mammals and other vertebrates is their size. On average, a mammal has a brain roughly twice as large as that of a bird of the same body size, and ten times as large as that of a reptile of the same body size.[[60]](#cite_note-Northcutt2002-60)

Size, however, is not the only difference: there are also substantial differences in shape. The hindbrain and midbrain of mammals are generally similar to those of other vertebrates, but dramatic differences appear in the forebrain, which is greatly enlarged and also altered in structure.[[61]](#cite_note-Barton-61) The cerebral cortex is the part of the brain that most strongly distinguishes mammals. In non-mammalian vertebrates, the surface of the [cerebrum](/wiki/Cerebrum "Cerebrum") is lined with a comparatively simple three-layered structure called the [pallium](/wiki/Pallium_(neuroanatomy) "Pallium (neuroanatomy)"). In mammals, the pallium evolves into a complex six-layered structure called [neocortex](/wiki/Neocortex "Neocortex") or *isocortex*.[[62]](#cite_note-Aboitiz-62) Several areas at the edge of the neocortex, including the hippocampus and [amygdala](/wiki/Amygdala "Amygdala"), are also much more extensively developed in mammals than in other vertebrates.[[61]](#cite_note-Barton-61)

The elaboration of the cerebral cortex carries with it changes to other brain areas. The [superior colliculus](/wiki/Superior_colliculus "Superior colliculus"), which plays a major role in visual control of behavior in most vertebrates, shrinks to a small size in mammals, and many of its functions are taken over by visual areas of the cerebral cortex.[[60]](#cite_note-Northcutt2002-60) The cerebellum of mammals contains a large portion (the [neocerebellum](/wiki/Neocerebellum "Neocerebellum")) dedicated to supporting the cerebral cortex, which has no counterpart in other vertebrates.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

In [placentals](/wiki/Placental "Placental"), there is a wide nerve tract connecting the cerebral hemispheres called the [corpus callosum](/wiki/Corpus_callosum "Corpus callosum").

The [brains of humans](/wiki/Human_brain "Human brain") and other [primates](/wiki/Primate "Primate") contain the same structures as the brains of other mammals, but are generally larger in proportion to body size.[[67]](#cite_note-Finlay-67) The [encephalization quotient](/wiki/Encephalization_quotient "Encephalization quotient") (EQ) is used to compare brain sizes across species. It takes into account the nonlinearity of the brain-to-body relationship.[[64]](#cite_note-Roth2005-64) Humans have an average EQ in the 7-to-8 range, while most other primates have an EQ in the 2-to-3 range. Dolphins have values higher than those of primates other than humans,[[65]](#cite_note-Marino-65) but nearly all other mammals have EQ values that are substantially lower.

Most of the enlargement of the primate brain comes from a massive expansion of the cerebral cortex, especially the [prefrontal cortex](/wiki/Prefrontal_cortex "Prefrontal cortex") and the parts of the cortex involved in [vision](/wiki/Visual_perception "Visual perception").[[68]](#cite_note-68) The visual processing network of primates includes at least 30 distinguishable brain areas, with a complex web of interconnections. It has been estimated that visual processing areas occupy more than half of the total surface of the primate neocortex.[[69]](#cite_note-Sereno1995-69) The [prefrontal cortex](/wiki/Prefrontal_cortex "Prefrontal cortex") carries out functions that include [planning](/wiki/Foresight_(psychology) "Foresight (psychology)"), [working memory](/wiki/Working_memory "Working memory"), [motivation](/wiki/Motivation "Motivation"), [attention](/wiki/Attention "Attention"), and [executive control](/wiki/Executive_functions "Executive functions"). It takes up a much larger proportion of the brain for primates than for other species, and an especially large fraction of the human brain.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

## Development

Main article: [Neural development](/wiki/Neural_development "Neural development")

[![Very simple drawing of the front end of a human embryo, showing each vesicle of the developing brain in a different color.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/6_week_embryo_brain.jpg/330px-6_week_embryo_brain.jpg)](/wiki/File:6_week_embryo_brain.jpg)

Brain of a human embryo in the sixth week of development

The brain develops in an intricately orchestrated sequence of stages.[[71]](#cite_note-Purves_1985-71) It changes in shape from a simple swelling at the front of the nerve cord in the earliest embryonic stages, to a complex array of areas and connections. Neurons are created in special zones that contain [stem cells](/wiki/Stem_cell "Stem cell"), and then migrate through the tissue to reach their ultimate locations. Once neurons have positioned themselves, their axons sprout and navigate through the brain, branching and extending as they go, until the tips reach their targets and form synaptic connections. In a number of parts of the nervous system, neurons and synapses are produced in excessive numbers during the early stages, and then the unneeded ones are pruned away.[[71]](#cite_note-Purves_1985-71)

For vertebrates, the early stages of neural development are similar across all species.[[71]](#cite_note-Purves_1985-71) As the embryo transforms from a round blob of cells into a wormlike structure, a narrow strip of [ectoderm](/wiki/Ectoderm "Ectoderm") running along the midline of the back is [induced](/wiki/Cellular_differentiation "Cellular differentiation") to become the [neural plate](/wiki/Neural_plate "Neural plate"), the precursor of the nervous system. The neural plate folds inward to form the [neural groove](/wiki/Neural_groove "Neural groove"), and then the lips that line the groove merge to enclose the [neural tube](/wiki/Neural_tube "Neural tube"), a hollow cord of cells with a fluid-filled ventricle at the center. At the front end, the ventricles and cord swell to form three vesicles that are the precursors of the [prosencephalon](/wiki/Forebrain "Forebrain") (forebrain), [mesencephalon](/wiki/Midbrain "Midbrain") (midbrain), and [rhombencephalon](/wiki/Hindbrain "Hindbrain") (hindbrain). At the next stage, the forebrain splits into two vesicles called the [telencephalon](/wiki/Cerebrum "Cerebrum") (which will contain the cerebral cortex, basal ganglia, and related structures) and the [diencephalon](/wiki/Diencephalon "Diencephalon") (which will contain the thalamus and hypothalamus). At about the same time, the hindbrain splits into the [metencephalon](/wiki/Metencephalon "Metencephalon") (which will contain the cerebellum and pons) and the [myelencephalon](/wiki/Myelencephalon "Myelencephalon") (which will contain the [medulla oblongata](/wiki/Medulla_oblongata "Medulla oblongata")). Each of these areas contains proliferative zones where neurons and glial cells are generated; the resulting cells then migrate, sometimes for long distances, to their final positions.[[71]](#cite_note-Purves_1985-71)

Once a neuron is in place, it extends dendrites and an axon into the area around it. Axons, because they commonly extend a great distance from the cell body and need to reach specific targets, grow in a particularly complex way. The tip of a growing axon consists of a blob of protoplasm called a [growth cone](/wiki/Growth_cone "Growth cone"), studded with chemical receptors. These receptors sense the local environment, causing the growth cone to be attracted or repelled by various cellular elements, and thus to be pulled in a particular direction at each point along its path. The result of this pathfinding process is that the growth cone navigates through the brain until it reaches its destination area, where other chemical cues cause it to begin generating synapses. Considering the entire brain, thousands of [genes](/wiki/Gene "Gene") create products that influence axonal pathfinding.[[71]](#cite_note-Purves_1985-71)

The synaptic network that finally emerges is only partly determined by genes, though. In many parts of the brain, axons initially "overgrow", and then are "pruned" by mechanisms that depend on neural activity.[[71]](#cite_note-Purves_1985-71) In the projection from the eye to the midbrain, for example, the structure in the adult contains a very precise mapping, connecting each point on the surface of the [retina](/wiki/Retina "Retina") to a corresponding point in a midbrain layer. In the first stages of development, each axon from the retina is guided to the right general vicinity in the midbrain by chemical cues, but then branches very profusely and makes initial contact with a wide swath of midbrain neurons. The retina, before birth, contains special mechanisms that cause it to generate waves of activity that originate spontaneously at a random point and then propagate slowly across the retinal layer. These waves are useful because they cause neighboring neurons to be active at the same time; that is, they produce a neural activity pattern that contains information about the spatial arrangement of the neurons. This information is exploited in the midbrain by a mechanism that causes synapses to weaken, and eventually vanish, if activity in an axon is not followed by activity of the target cell. The result of this sophisticated process is a gradual tuning and tightening of the map, leaving it finally in its precise adult form.[[72]](#cite_note-Wong-72)

Similar things happen in other brain areas: an initial synaptic matrix is generated as a result of genetically determined chemical guidance, but then gradually refined by activity-dependent mechanisms, partly driven by internal dynamics, partly by external sensory inputs. In some cases, as with the retina-midbrain system, activity patterns depend on mechanisms that operate only in the developing brain, and apparently exist solely to guide development.[[72]](#cite_note-Wong-72)

In humans and many other mammals, new neurons are created mainly before birth, and the infant brain contains substantially more neurons than the adult brain.[[71]](#cite_note-Purves_1985-71) There are, however, a few areas where new neurons continue to be generated throughout life. The two areas for which adult [neurogenesis](/wiki/Neurogenesis "Neurogenesis") is well established are the olfactory bulb, which is involved in the sense of smell, and the [dentate gyrus](/wiki/Dentate_gyrus "Dentate gyrus") of the hippocampus, where there is evidence that the new neurons play a role in storing newly acquired memories. With these exceptions, however, the set of neurons that is present in early childhood is the set that is present for life. Glial cells are different: as with most types of cells in the body, they are generated throughout the lifespan.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

There has long been debate about whether the qualities of [mind](/wiki/Mind "Mind"), personality, and intelligence can be attributed to [heredity or to upbringing](/wiki/Nature_versus_nurture "Nature versus nurture").[[74]](#cite_note-74) Although many details remain to be settled, neuroscience shows that both factors are important. Genes determine both the general form of the brain and how it reacts to experience, but experience is required to refine the matrix of synaptic connections, resulting in greatly increased complexity. The presence or absence of experience is critical at key periods of development.[[75]](#cite_note-75) Additionally, the quantity and quality of experience are important. For example, animals raised in [enriched environments](/wiki/Environmental_enrichment_(neural) "Environmental enrichment (neural)") demonstrate thick cerebral cortices, indicating a high density of synaptic connections, compared to animals with restricted levels of stimulation.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

## Physiology

The functions of the brain depend on the ability of neurons to transmit electrochemical signals to other cells, and their ability to respond appropriately to electrochemical signals received from other cells. The [electrical properties](/wiki/Membrane_potential "Membrane potential") of neurons are controlled by a wide variety of biochemical and metabolic processes, most notably the interactions between neurotransmitters and receptors that take place at synapses.[[8]](#cite_note-Kandel_2000-8)

### Neurotransmitters and receptors

[Neurotransmitters](/wiki/Neurotransmitter "Neurotransmitter") are chemicals that are released at synapses when the local membrane is depolarised and [Ca2+](/wiki/Calcium "Calcium") enters into the cell, typically when an action potential arrives at the synapse – neurotransmitters attach themselves to receptor molecules on the membrane of the synapse's target cell (or cells), and thereby alter the electrical or chemical properties of the receptor molecules. With few exceptions, each neuron in the brain releases the same chemical neurotransmitter, or combination of neurotransmitters, at all the synaptic connections it makes with other neurons; this rule is known as [Dale's principle](/wiki/Dale%27s_principle "Dale's principle").[[8]](#cite_note-Kandel_2000-8) Thus, a neuron can be characterized by the neurotransmitters that it releases. The great majority of [psychoactive drugs](/wiki/Psychoactive_drug "Psychoactive drug") exert their effects by altering specific neurotransmitter systems. This applies to drugs such as [cannabinoids](/wiki/Cannabinoids "Cannabinoids"), [nicotine](/wiki/Nicotine "Nicotine"), [heroin](/wiki/Heroin "Heroin"), [cocaine](/wiki/Cocaine "Cocaine"), [alcohol](/wiki/Alcohol_(drug) "Alcohol (drug)"), [fluoxetine](/wiki/Fluoxetine "Fluoxetine"), [chlorpromazine](/wiki/Chlorpromazine "Chlorpromazine"), and many others.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

The two neurotransmitters that are most widely found in the vertebrate brain are [glutamate](/wiki/Glutamic_acid "Glutamic acid"), which almost always exerts excitatory effects on target neurons, and [gamma-aminobutyric acid](/wiki/Gamma-aminobutyric_acid "Gamma-aminobutyric acid") (GABA), which is almost always inhibitory. Neurons using these transmitters can be found in nearly every part of the brain.[[78]](#cite_note-78) Because of their ubiquity, drugs that act on glutamate or GABA tend to have broad and powerful effects. Some [general anesthetics](/wiki/General_anaesthetic "General anaesthetic") act by reducing the effects of glutamate; most tranquilizers exert their sedative effects by enhancing the effects of GABA.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

There are dozens of other chemical neurotransmitters that are used in more limited areas of the brain, often areas dedicated to a particular function. [Serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin"), for example—the primary target of many [antidepressant drugs](/wiki/Antidepressant "Antidepressant") and many dietary aids—comes exclusively from a small brainstem area called the [raphe nuclei](/wiki/Raphe_nuclei "Raphe nuclei").[[80]](#cite_note-80) [Norepinephrine](/wiki/Norepinephrine "Norepinephrine"), which is involved in arousal, comes exclusively from a nearby small area called the [locus coeruleus](/wiki/Locus_coeruleus "Locus coeruleus").[[81]](#cite_note-Mehler-81) Other neurotransmitters such as [acetylcholine](/wiki/Acetylcholine "Acetylcholine") and [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine") have multiple sources in the brain but are not as ubiquitously distributed as glutamate and GABA.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

### Electrical activity

[![Graph showing 16 voltage traces going across the page from left to right, each showing a different signal. At the middle of the page all of the traces abruptly begin to show sharp jerky spikes, which continue to the end of the plot.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Spike-waves.png/250px-Spike-waves.png)](/wiki/File:Spike-waves.png)

Brain electrical activity recorded from a human patient during an [epileptic seizure](/wiki/Epilepsy "Epilepsy")

As a side effect of the electrochemical processes used by neurons for signaling, brain tissue generates electric fields when it is active. When large numbers of neurons show synchronized activity, the electric fields that they generate can be large enough to detect outside the skull, using [electroencephalography](/wiki/Electroencephalography "Electroencephalography") (EEG)[[83]](#cite_note-Niedermeyer-83) or [magnetoencephalography](/wiki/Magnetoencephalography "Magnetoencephalography") (MEG). EEG recordings, along with recordings made from electrodes implanted inside the brains of animals such as rats, show that the brain of a living animal is constantly active, even during sleep.[[84]](#cite_note-Buzsaki-84) Each part of the brain shows a mixture of rhythmic and nonrhythmic activity, which may vary according to behavioral state. In mammals, the cerebral cortex tends to show large slow [delta waves](/wiki/Delta_wave "Delta wave") during sleep, faster [alpha waves](/wiki/Alpha_wave "Alpha wave") when the animal is awake but inattentive, and chaotic-looking irregular activity when the animal is actively engaged in a task, called [beta](/wiki/Beta_wave "Beta wave") and [gamma waves](/wiki/Gamma_wave "Gamma wave"). During an [epileptic seizure](/wiki/Epilepsy "Epilepsy"), the brain's inhibitory control mechanisms fail to function and electrical activity rises to pathological levels, producing EEG traces that show large wave and spike patterns not seen in a healthy brain. Relating these population-level patterns to the computational functions of individual neurons is a major focus of current research in [neurophysiology](/wiki/Neurophysiology "Neurophysiology").[[84]](#cite_note-Buzsaki-84)

### Metabolism

All vertebrates have a [blood–brain barrier](/wiki/Blood%E2%80%93brain_barrier "Blood–brain barrier") that allows metabolism inside the brain to operate differently from metabolism in other parts of the body. The [neurovascular unit](/wiki/Neurovascular_unit "Neurovascular unit") regulates cerebral blood flow so that activated neurons can be supplied with energy. [Glial cells](/wiki/Neuroglia "Neuroglia") play a major role in brain metabolism by controlling the chemical composition of the fluid that surrounds neurons, including levels of ions and nutrients.[[85]](#cite_note-Nieuwenhuys-85)

Brain tissue consumes a large amount of energy in proportion to its volume, hence large brains place severe metabolic demands on animals. The need to limit body weight in order, for example, to fly, has apparently led to selection for a reduction of brain size in some species, such as [bats](/wiki/Bats "Bats").[[86]](#cite_note-86) Most of the brain's energy consumption goes into sustaining the electric charge ([membrane potential](/wiki/Membrane_potential "Membrane potential")) of neurons.[[85]](#cite_note-Nieuwenhuys-85) Most vertebrate species devote between 2% and 8% of basal metabolism to the brain. In primates, however, the percentage is much higher—in humans it rises to 20–25%.[[87]](#cite_note-87) The energy consumption of the brain does not vary greatly over time, but active regions of the cerebral cortex consume somewhat more energy than inactive regions; this forms the basis for the functional brain imaging methods of [PET](/wiki/Brain_positron_emission_tomography "Brain positron emission tomography"), [fMRI](/wiki/FMRI "FMRI"),[[88]](#cite_note-88) and [NIRS](/wiki/Near_infrared_spectroscopy "Near infrared spectroscopy").[[89]](#cite_note-89) The brain typically gets most of its energy from oxygen-dependent metabolism of [glucose](/wiki/Glucose "Glucose") (i.e., blood sugar),[[85]](#cite_note-Nieuwenhuys-85) but [ketones](/wiki/Ketone_bodies "Ketone bodies") provide a major alternative source, together with contributions from medium chain [fatty acids](/wiki/Fatty_acid "Fatty acid") ([caprylic](/wiki/Caprylic_acid "Caprylic acid") and [heptanoic](/wiki/Heptanoic_acid "Heptanoic acid") acids),[[90]](#cite_note-Ebert_2003-90)[[91]](#cite_note-Marin_Valencia_2013-91) [lactate](/wiki/Lactic_acid "Lactic acid"),[[92]](#cite_note-Boumezbeur_2010-92) [acetate](/wiki/Acetate "Acetate"),[[93]](#cite_note-Deelchand_2009-93) and possibly [amino acids](/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid").[[94]](#cite_note-94)

## Function

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Model_of_Cerebellar_Perceptron.jpg/250px-Model_of_Cerebellar_Perceptron.jpg)](/wiki/File:Model_of_Cerebellar_Perceptron.jpg)

Model of a neural circuit in the cerebellum, as proposed by [James S. Albus](/wiki/James_S._Albus "James S. Albus")

Information from the sense organs is collected in the brain. There it is used to determine what actions the organism is to take. The brain [processes](/wiki/Multisensory_integration "Multisensory integration") the raw data to extract information about the structure of the environment. Next it combines the processed information with information about the current needs of the animal and with memory of past circumstances. Finally, on the basis of the results, it generates motor response patterns. These signal-processing tasks require intricate interplay between a variety of functional subsystems.[[95]](#cite_note-CarewCh1-95)

The function of the brain is to provide coherent control over the actions of an animal. A centralized brain allows groups of muscles to be co-activated in complex patterns; it also allows stimuli impinging on one part of the body to evoke responses in other parts, and it can prevent different parts of the body from acting at cross-purposes to each other.[[95]](#cite_note-CarewCh1-95)

### Perception

[![Drawing showing the ear, inner ear, and brain areas involved in hearing. A series of light blue arrows shows the flow of signals through the system.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Hearing_mechanics_cropped.jpg/250px-Hearing_mechanics_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hearing_mechanics_cropped.jpg)

Diagram of signal processing in the [auditory system](/wiki/Auditory_system "Auditory system")

The human brain is provided with information about light, sound, the chemical composition of the atmosphere, temperature, the position of the body in space ([proprioception](/wiki/Proprioception "Proprioception")), the chemical composition of the bloodstream, and more. In other animals additional senses are present, such as the [infrared heat-sense of snakes](/wiki/Infrared_sensing_in_snakes "Infrared sensing in snakes"), the [magnetic field sense](/wiki/Magnetoception "Magnetoception") of some birds, or the [electric field sense](/wiki/Electroreception "Electroreception") mainly seen in aquatic animals.

Each sensory system begins with specialized receptor cells,[[8]](#cite_note-Kandel_2000-8) such as [photoreceptor cells](/wiki/Photoreceptor_cell "Photoreceptor cell") in the [retina](/wiki/Retina "Retina") of the [eye](/wiki/Eye "Eye"), or vibration-sensitive [hair cells](/wiki/Hair_cell "Hair cell") in the [cochlea](/wiki/Cochlea "Cochlea") of the [ear](/wiki/Ear "Ear"). The axons of sensory receptor cells travel into the spinal cord or brain, where they transmit their signals to a [first-order sensory nucleus](/wiki/Sensory_system "Sensory system") dedicated to one specific [sensory modality](/wiki/Stimulus_modality "Stimulus modality"). This primary sensory nucleus sends information to higher-order sensory areas that are dedicated to the same modality. Eventually, via a way-station in the [thalamus](/wiki/Thalamus "Thalamus"), the signals are sent to the cerebral cortex, where they are processed to extract the relevant features, and [integrated](/wiki/Multisensory_integration "Multisensory integration") with signals coming from other sensory systems.[[8]](#cite_note-Kandel_2000-8)

### Motor control

[Motor systems](/wiki/Motor_system "Motor system") are areas of the brain that are involved in [initiating body movements](/wiki/Motor_control "Motor control"), that is, in activating muscles. Except for the muscles that control the eye, which are driven by nuclei in the midbrain, all the voluntary muscles in the body are directly innervated by [motor neurons](/wiki/Motor_neuron "Motor neuron") in the spinal cord and hindbrain.[[8]](#cite_note-Kandel_2000-8) Spinal motor neurons are controlled both by neural circuits intrinsic to the spinal cord, and by inputs that descend from the brain. The intrinsic spinal circuits implement many [reflex](/wiki/Reflex "Reflex") responses, and contain [pattern generators](/wiki/Central_pattern_generator "Central pattern generator") for rhythmic movements such as [walking](/wiki/Walking "Walking") or [swimming](/wiki/Aquatic_locomotion "Aquatic locomotion"). The descending connections from the brain allow for more sophisticated control.[[8]](#cite_note-Kandel_2000-8)

The brain contains several motor areas that project directly to the spinal cord. At the lowest level are motor areas in the medulla and pons, which control stereotyped movements such as walking, [breathing](/wiki/Breathing "Breathing"), or [swallowing](/wiki/Swallowing "Swallowing"). At a higher level are areas in the midbrain, such as the [red nucleus](/wiki/Red_nucleus "Red nucleus"), which is responsible for coordinating movements of the arms and legs. At a higher level yet is the [primary motor cortex](/wiki/Primary_motor_cortex "Primary motor cortex"), a strip of tissue located at the posterior edge of the frontal lobe. The primary motor cortex sends projections to the subcortical motor areas, but also sends a massive projection directly to the spinal cord, through the [pyramidal tract](/wiki/Pyramidal_tract "Pyramidal tract"). This direct corticospinal projection allows for precise voluntary control of the fine details of movements. Other motor-related brain areas exert secondary effects by projecting to the primary motor areas. Among the most important secondary areas are the [premotor cortex](/wiki/Premotor_cortex "Premotor cortex"), [supplementary motor area](/wiki/Supplementary_motor_area "Supplementary motor area"), [basal ganglia](/wiki/Basal_ganglia "Basal ganglia"), and [cerebellum](/wiki/Cerebellum "Cerebellum").[[8]](#cite_note-Kandel_2000-8) In addition to all of the above, the brain and spinal cord contain extensive circuitry to control the [autonomic nervous system](/wiki/Autonomic_nervous_system "Autonomic nervous system") which controls the movement of the [smooth muscle](/wiki/Smooth_muscle "Smooth muscle") of the body.[[8]](#cite_note-Kandel_2000-8)

Major areas involved in controlling movement

| Area | Location | Function |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Ventral horn](/wiki/Anterior_horn_of_spinal_cord "Anterior horn of spinal cord") | Spinal cord | Contains motor neurons that directly activate muscles[[96]](#cite_note-96) |
| [Oculomotor nuclei](/wiki/Oculomotor_nucleus "Oculomotor nucleus") | Midbrain | Contains motor neurons that directly activate the eye muscles[[97]](#cite_note-97) |
| [Cerebellum](/wiki/Cerebellum "Cerebellum") | Hindbrain | Calibrates precision and timing of movements[[8]](#cite_note-Kandel_2000-8) |
